---
title: "Linear maps"
description: "Linear maps, the vector space L(U,W), and products of linear maps — an augmented summary of Axler's LADR §3.A."
date: "2026-06-03"
date-modified: "2026-06-18"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Section 3.A of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

As in the previous section, we assume that $\mb{F}\in\{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}\}$.

## Linear map
A function $T:V\to W$ between vector spaces is called a **linear map** if it satisfies two properties:

1. It is *additive* in that it commutes with vector addition: $T(u+v)=Tu+Tv$.
2. It is *homogeneous* in that it commutes with scalar multiplication: $T(\lambda v)=\lambda Tv$.

<details class="example">
<summary>Identity map</summary>

The trivial example is the **zero map** $0$, which maps all vectors to the zero vector: $0v=0$^[Now there are three uses of the symbol "$0$", for the vector, the scalar, or the map.]. There is also the **identity map** $I$ that maps each vector to itself.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Differentiation and integration maps</summary>

Because of how differentiation works for sums and scalar multiples of functions, the map $D\in\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R}),\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R}))$ given by

$$Dp=p'$$

is linear. Additionally, a definite integral 

$$Tp=\int_0^1p(x)dx$$

is a linear map from $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ to $\mathbb{R}$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Backward shift map</summary>

Consider the *backward shift* map $T:\mb{F}^\infty\to\mb{F}^\infty$ defined as

$$T(x_1,x_2,x_3,\cdots)=(x_2,x_3,\cdots).$$

As a "destroy-and-shift" operation, this doesn't look very linear, but we have

$$
\begin{gather}
T(x_1+x'_1,x_2+x'_2,x_3+x'_3,\cdots)=(x_2+x'_2,x_3+x'_3,\cdots)=(x_2,x_3,\cdots)+(x'_2,x'_3,\cdots)\\
T(\lambda x_1,\lambda x_2, \lambda x_3,\cdots)=(\lambda x_2, \lambda x_3,\cdots)=\lambda(x_2, x_3,\cdots)
\end{gather}
$$

as required of a linear operator.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Homogeneous but not linear map from $\mathbb{R}^2$ to $\mathbb{R}$</summary>

The magnitude function $\phi:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}$ is defined as

$$\phi(x,y)=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}.$$

This function is homogeneous because

$$\phi(ax,ay)=\sqrt{a^2x^2+a^2y^2}=a\sqrt{x^2+y^2},$$

but not additive as

$$\phi(x_1+x_2,y_1+y_2)=\sqrt{(x_1+x_2)^2+(y_1+y_2)^2}\neq\phi(x_1,y_1)+\phi(x_2,y_2)$$

in general.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Additive but not linear map from $\mathbb{C}$ to $\mathbb{C}$</summary>

The complex conjugate function $\phi:\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}$ is defined as $\phi(z)=\overline{z}$.

It is additive because $\overline{z_1+z_2}=\overline{z_1}+\overline{z_2}$, but not homogeneous as 

$$\overline{az}=\overline{a}\ \overline{z}\neq a\overline{z}$$

for any complex non-real $a$.

</details>

#### Expressing linear maps from $\mb{F}^n$ to $\mb{F}^m$
:::{#thm-}
Every linear map from $\mb{F}^n$ to $\mb{F}^m$ acts as

$$T(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=(A_{1,1}x_1+\cdots+A_{1,n}x_n,\cdots,A_{m,1}x_1+\cdots+A_{m,n}x_n).$$

:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Consider an arbitrary linear map $T:\mb{F}^n\to\mb{F}^m$.

Denoting the $i$th standard basis by $e_i$, there exist scalars $A_{j,i}$ such that each standard basis vector of $\mb{F}^n$ is mapped to a linear combination of the standard basis of $\mb{F}^m$ as

$$Te_i=\sum_{j=1}^mA_{j,i}e_j=(A_{1,i},\cdots,A_{m,i}).$$

(This is an abuse of notation, because we are using $e_i$ for the standard basis vectors of both $\mb{F}^n$ and $\mb{F}^m$, which have different lengths.)

Then the action of $T$ on a general vector $(x_1,\cdots,x_n)\in\mb{F}^n$ can be represented as

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_1,\cdots,x_n)&=T(\sum_{i=1}^nx_ie_i)\\
&=\sum_{i=1}^nx_iTe_i\\
&=\sum_{i=1}^nx_i(\sum_{j=1}^mA_{j,i}e_j)\\
&=\sum_{j=1}^m(\sum_{i=1}^nA_{j,i}x_i)e_j\\
&=(A_{1,1}x_1+\cdots+A_{1,n}x_n,\cdots,A_{m,1}x_1+\cdots+A_{m,n}x_n),
\end{aligned}
$$

as required.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Map from $\mathbb{R}^3$ to $\mathbb{R}^2$</summary>

The map $T:mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2$ defined as

$$T(x_1,x_2,x_3)=(3x_1+2x_2-x_3,x_2+3x_3)$$

is a linear map. Using the previous notation, we have

$$A_{1,1}=3,\ A_{1,2}=2,\ A_{1,3}=-1,\ A_{2,1}=0,\ A_{2,2}=1,\ A_{2,3}=3,$$

which can be organised into a $2\times 3$ matrix as

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
3&2&-1\\
0&1&3
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

This matrix specifies the action of the linear map.

</details>

#### Linear map for 1-dimensional space
:::{#thm-1d-scalar}
For a 1-dimensional vector space $V$, any linear map $T:V\to V$ is a multiplication by some scalar.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Every vector in $V$ is a scalar multiple of some non-zero $v\in V$, and $T$ is homogeneous.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $v'$ be any non-zero vector in $V$. Then $v'$ is a basis of $V$, so there is $\lambda\in\mb{F}$ such that $Tv'=\lambda v'$. For any $av'\in V$, the homogeneity property of $T$ leads to

$$T(av')=aTv'=a(\lambda v')=\lambda(av'),$$

and so $Tv=\lambda v$ for all $v\in V$.

</details>

#### Mapping zero
:::{#thm-maps-zero}
All linear maps send zero vectors to zero vectors.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Use additivity.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

By the additivity property of $T$, we have

$$T0=T(0+0)=T0+T0\implies T0=0.$$

</details>

#### Linear independence under mapping
:::{#thm-preimage-independence}
If $Tv_1,\cdots,Tv_n$ is linearly independent, then so is $v_1,\cdots,v_n$.
:::

:::{.informal}
We cannot send a linearly dependent set of vectors to a linearly independent set by a linear map. So there is a restriction as to what linear maps can do. 
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
If a linear combination vanishes, so does its image by the previous result.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $Tv_1,\cdots,Tv_n$ is linearly independent, and the scalars are such that $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n=0$. Then we have

$$0=T(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)=a_1Tv_1+\cdots+a_nTv_n,$$

and so $a_i=0$ by the linear independence of $Tv_1,\cdots,Tv_n$.

</details>

The converse does not hold; that is, a linearly independent set of vectors may not be sent to another linearly independent set under a linear map. For example, the zero map sends any set of vectors to the linearly dependent set $\{0\}$. A less trivial case involves the vectors

$$(1,0,0),(0,1,0),(1,1,1)$$

and the projection linear map $T:(x,y,z)\mapsto(x,y)$. So *even after* removing the zero vectors in the image, linear maps do not "preserve" linear independence.

###### Impossible maps
:::{#thm-impossible-maps}
If $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ is linearly dependent and $W$ is not trivial, there *always* exists a set $w_1,\cdots,w_n\in W$ such that no linear maps satisfy $Tu_i=w_i$.

(From the previous theorem, we know that this result holds if $\dim W\geq n$, by setting the $w_i$s to be linearly independent.)
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
By linear dependence, there exist $a_i$ not all zero such that

$$a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n=0.$$

So *all* linear maps $T:U\to W$ must satisfy

$$0=T(a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n)=a_1Tu_1+\cdots+a_nTu_n=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n,$$

and our job is to find $w_i$s that fails this. 
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

The choice is quite obvious. Without loss of generality, let $a_1$ be a non-zero scalar. Let $w_1$ be a non-zero vector of $W$, and set all other $w_i$s to be the zero vector. Then the above expression reduces to 

$$0=a_1w_1.$$ 

This is impossible because both $a_1$ and $w_1$ are not zero, and so $a_1w_1$ cannot be zero.

</details>

#### Mapping basis
:::{#thm-basis-map-exists}
Consider two vector spaces $U$ and $W$, where $U$ is finite-dimensional. Let $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ be a basis of $U$. Then for *any* set of vectors $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ in $W$, there exists a linear map $T:U\to W$ such that $Tu_i=w_i$.
:::

:::{.informal}
Unlike with a linearly dependent set, a suitable linear map can send a linearly independent set of vectors to an arbitrary set of vectors in the image.

(Although we wrote that $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ is a *basis* of $U$, nothing hangs on $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ spanning $U$, other than the domain of $T$.)
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Given that $Tu_i=w_i$, there is only one definition of $T:U\to W$ that makes sense.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

We need to first define a map from $U$ to $W$, and then show that this map is linear. Expressing each vector of $U$ as $u=a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n$, we may define $T$ as

$$Tu=T(a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n)=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n.$$

This is well-defined because the linear independence of $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ guarantees that $a_1,\cdots,a_n$ are uniquely given. Clearly, $Tu_i=w_i$ is satisfied.

We have to check that $T$ is linear. Let

$$
\begin{gather}
u=a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n\\
u'=a'_1u_1+\cdots+a'_nu_n
\end{gather}
$$

be an arbitrary pair of vectors in $U$. Then we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(u+u')&=T((a_1+a'_1)u_1+\cdots+(a_n+a'_n)u_n)\\
&=(a_1+a'_1)w_1+\cdots+(a_n+a'_n)w_n\\
&=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n+a'_1w_1+\cdots+a'_nw_n\\
&=Tu+Tu',
\end{aligned}
$$

which verifies that $T$ is additive. Similarly, we can show that $T$ is homogeneous.

</details>

:::{#thm-basis-map-unique}
The linear map $T$ defined above is the *only* linear map from $U$ to $W$ that satisfies $Tu_i=w_i$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $T:U\to W$ is a linear map such that $Tu_i=w_i$. Then by additivity and homogeneity, the action of such $T$ on any $a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n\in U$ must be given by

$$T(a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n)=a_1Tu_1+\cdots+a_nTu_n=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n.$$

This is exactly the linear map defined above.

</details>

In summary, we have seen that a linear map is *specifiable* by its action on a basis of its domain, and *uniquely* so.

###### Extending a linear map
Suppose that $U$ is a proper subspace of $V$, and $S:U\to W$ is a linear map. We may wonder if $S$ can be extended to a linear map $T:V\to W$, such that $Su=Tu$ for all $u\in U$. This is obviously possible if $S$ is the zero map, so let us suppose that $S\neq0$.

:::{#thm-extension-zero-fails}
Our first idea might be to send all vectors in $V-U$ to the zero vector. Unfortunately, this is not a linear map.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Consider $u+v$ where $u\in U$ is not mapped to $0$ while $v\in V-U$ is.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Since $S$ is not a zero map, we can find $u\in U$ such that $Su\neq 0$. Let $v$ be a vector in $V-U$, and let us consider $u+v\in V$.

If $u+v\in U$, then we have $v\in U$, which is a contradiction. Therefore, $u+v\in V-U$ as well. 

Now, let $T$ be an extension of $S$ with $Tx=0$ for all $x\in V-U$. Then we have $T(u+v)=0$ but

$$Tu+Tv=Tu\neq0,$$

violating additivity. Therefore, $T$ is not a linear map.

</details>

:::{#thm-extension-exists}
Despite the above result, there does exist an extension of $S$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
As it turns out, we just had to send the additional basis vectors to zero, not the whole vectors.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ be a basis of $U$, and suppose that appending $v_1,\cdots,v_m$ to the set makes it a basis of $V$. We know that specifying the images of the basis vectors completely specifies a linear map, so let us define a linear map $T$ as 

$$Tu_i=Su_i\quad\text{and}\quad Tv_i=0.$$ 

This satisfies $Tu=Su$ for all $u\in U$. 

</details>

Actually, we could have chosen any vector as $Tv_i$, not just the zero vector.

## Set of linear maps
#### As vector space
Let $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ denote the set of all linear maps from $U$ to $W$, where $U$ and $W$ are vector spaces over $\mb{F}$. Let us equip it with addition and scalar multiplication defined as

$$
\begin{gather}
(S+T)v=Sv+Tv\\
(\lambda S)v=\lambda(Sv),
\end{gather}
$$

where $\lambda\in\mb{F}$. 

:::{#thm-}
$\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ is a vector space under these operations.
:::

###### Infinite dimensions
As a vector space, we can speak of the dimension of $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$. (Let's not think too hard about what the basis looks like for now.) 

:::{#thm-luw-infinite}
$\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ is always infinite-dimensional whenever $W$ is infinite-dimensional and $U\neq\{0\}$ is finite-dimensional.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
From a previous result, we know that $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ is infinite-dimensional if there exists a countably infinite sequence $T_1,T_2,\cdots\in\mathcal{L}(U,W)$, where $T_1,\cdots,T_n$ is linearly independent for all $n$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $w_1,w_2,\cdots$ be an infinite linearly independent set in $W$. (This can be constructed by choosing as the $i$th element a vector outside the span of the first $i-1$ vectors.) Let $u_1,\cdots,u_m$ be a basis of $U$. Then we may define a sequence of linear maps $T_i:U\to W$ as

$$
T_iu_1=w_i\quad\text{and}\quad T_iu_j=0\text{ for }j=2,\cdots,m.
$$

Now consider the subset $T_1,\cdots,T_n$, and suppose that $b_1T_1+\cdots+b_nT_n=0$. Then for $u=au_1$ where $a$ is non-zero, we must have 

$$
\begin{aligned}
0&=(b_1T_1+\cdots+b_nT_n)u\\
&=b_1T_1u+\cdots+b_nT_nu\\
&=ab_1w_1+\cdots+ab_nw_n.
\end{aligned}
$$

By the linear independence of the $w_i$s, this implies that the $b_i$s are all zero. Therefore, $T_1,\cdots,T_n$ is linearly independent for any $n$, and so $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ is infinite-dimensional.

</details>

#### Product of linear maps
We call the composition of linear maps as their **product**. So if $T\in\mathcal{L}(U,V)$ and $S\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)$, then their product $ST\in\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ acts as

$$(ST)u=S(Tu).$$

:::{#thm-}
Like all function compositions, this product is associative: 

$$(T_1T_2)T_3=T_1(T_2T_3).$$

Also, the product distributes over the addition as

$$(S_1+S_2)T=S_1T+S_2T\quad\text{and}\quad S(T_1+T_2)=ST_1+ST_2.$$

:::

###### Failure of commutativity
:::{#thm-product-noncommutative}
The product is clearly not commutative when different vector spaces are involved. But even for operators (map of a vector space to itself), commutativity fails if $\dim V\geq 2$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $v_1$ and $v_2$ be two linearly independent vectors of $V$, i.e. non-zero vectors that are not scalar multiples of each other. Let $T:V\to V$ be any linear map that satisfies 

$$Tv_1=0\quad\text{and}\quad Tv_2=v_1,$$

and let $S:V\to V$ be any linear map that satisfies

$$Sv_1=v_2\quad\text{and}\quad Sv_2=v_1.$$

Then we have $(TS)v_1=v_1$ but $(ST)v_1=0$, so $TS\neq ST$.

</details>
